blocks.content: The house went quiet after eleven.
Lights out one by one. Sophie's room first, then Dean's. Cade's light stayed on until almost midnight — Julian could see it from the study window, a thin line under the curtain.
Wren's light went out at eleven thirty.

He mapped everything he had — the bonfire, the bracket, the booking trail, the call to Price, the reaction to Gideon Price's name, the walks on the beach, the six minutes at the railing. He mapped what Davenport had and what Davenport didn't have yet. He mapped what the tox screen would show and what it wouldn't be able to prove on its own.

He mapped Wren. What she had noticed — someone missing from the table during the evening window. She didn't know whose absence it was yet. But she would. Wren Cole was the kind of person who eventually arrived at the thing they were trying to remember.
When she does — that's the piece I need. That's the one thing I don't have. A witness inside the house for the evening window.

The circle he kept coming back to had a name in it now. He'd written it finally, after fighting it all day, because the evidence wasn't complete but the direction was and he needed to see it on paper.
He looked at it for a long time.
Then he turned the page over.
